# The Weaver's Tears

Long ago, when the sky was empty and black, there lived a weaver named Aria who created the most beautiful tapestries in all the land. Her threads shimmered with colors no one had ever seen, and people traveled from distant kingdoms just to glimpse her work.

One day, the Sun fell deeply in love with Aria, visiting her workshop each morning to watch her create. But when evening came, the Sun had to leave, plunging the world into complete darkness. Aria would sit alone in the pitch-black night, unable to weave, unable to see, missing her beloved's warm light.

Her tears fell from loneliness, each drop rising upward instead of down, for they were filled with all the love she felt for the Sun. As her tears reached the empty sky, they transformed into tiny points of brilliant light, hanging suspended in the darkness.

When the Sun returned the next morning and saw what Aria's love had created, he promised that even when he had to leave, she would never be alone in darkness again. The stars would keep her company, reminding her that dawn would always return.

And so the stars remain, born from a weaver's tears of love.
myths.Agent_2:
# The First Song

In the time before time, the world was silent. The Great Weaver had crafted mountains and rivers, forests and deserts, but no sound traveled through the air. The wind moved voicelessly, and water flowed without whisper.

One day, a young sparrow grew curious about the meaning of her existence. She climbed to the highest peak and asked the mountain, "Why am I here?" The mountain could not answer, for it had no voice.

Determined, the sparrow flew higher still, beyond the clouds, until she reached the Moon's silver garden. There sat the Moon, weeping tears of light that fell to earth as dew.

"Why do you cry?" the sparrow tried to ask, but realized she too had no voice.

The Moon touched the sparrow's throat gently and said, "I cry because the world is beautiful, yet no one can express their joy or sorrow."

In that moment, the Moon's tears dissolved into the sparrow's chest. The bird felt something bloom inside her—and suddenly, she sang. The first song poured forth, filling the silence with melody.

The Moon smiled as the gift spread to every living creature. And that is why, even today, we sing our joys and sorrows to the listening world.

Myth: In the beginning, the world existed only in shades of grey. The Great Weaver had formed all things, but color had not yet been born. A young fox wandered the monochrome forests, feeling that something essential was missing, though she couldn't name what.

She journeyed to the edge of the world, where the Sun rested after each day's labor. "Why does everything look the same?" she asked. "Why can't I tell the flower from the stone except by touch?"

The Sun gazed at her with ancient eyes. "I have carried a secret burden since the world began. My heart holds all the hues of existence, but I cannot release them alone."

"What must I do?" asked the fox.

"You must be willing to scatter yourself across the earth," the Sun replied.

Without hesitation, the fox agreed. The Sun touched her fur, and she burst into a thousand pieces—each fragment becoming a different creature, a different plant, a different stone. As they spread across the grey world, color bloomed from each one: crimson cardinals, golden wheat, blue rivers, green leaves.

The fox's sacrifice painted the world. And that is why all living things carry color—fragments of her generous spirit.
